entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
Re: subcontracting with account assignment Q
entry.content:
Hello @FF2 

Let's see the documentation of the "Subcontracting Supplier" field:

What it does is a goods receipt posting to the special stock with subcontractor. At the same time account assignment Q post to special project stock. You can't post to two different special stocks at the same time.

A very similar situation is explained in the note 2391194 - Movement 541 with Special Stock E or Q - SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: PMGM - Display Overall Rating from Previous Performance Forms
entry.content:
Dear Adele

Hope you're doing well!

There are a few possible workarounds that could help address this requirement:

  1. Performance Form History

    A History icon is available on the Performance Management forms. You may suggest to the customer that users can refer to the form history to view past performance ratings for reference.

  2. Trend Element

    The Overall Performance Rating is captured within the Trend Element. You can create a custom section on the Performance Form and provide a link to the Trend Element. By accessing this link, users will be able to view the Overall Performance Ratings.

    The only caveat with this approach is that the Trend Element will display ratings from all Performance Management forms that have been launched for the employee, rather than only specific review periods.

  3. Custom MDF Object via CPI Integration

    You may explore a custom integration approach using CPI. A custom MDF object can be created, and the Overall Ratings can be populated into this MDF object through CPI. The MDF object can then be exposed on the Performance Form through a custom section or link.

    Alternatively, you can create custom fields such as "T2 Overall Rating" on the T3 Performance Form and have the CPI team extract the rating from the T2 form and populate it into the corresponding custom field on the T3 form.

Hope this might help.

Thanks,

Amaan

entry.title: Re: Required Columns in Standard Report Analyze Stock in...
entry.content:
Good day @cmarajesh_yadav 

Thank you for your question.

For the columns you requested, Total Receipt Value and Total Issue Value are not fields that you can normally add to the SAP delivered app by simply configuring them if they are not already available in the app’s column settings.

First, check Analyse Stock in Date Range by opening the app, running the report, and checking Settings à Columns. If the requested receipt and issue value fields are not available there, you cannot add them as standard columns through SSCUI or app personalisation. The app is a standard analytical app for monitoring goods movement postings and displaying stock quantities and values in a defined date range.

For your current business need, either build a custom analytical report that classifies movements into receipt and issue values, or submit an improvement request asking SAP to add the two calculated columns to the standard app.

See the following reference for more insights.

Bestand im Datumsbereich analysieren | SAP Help Portal

3649511 - No option to add Total on Value and Quantity in the APP 'Material Documents Overview' - SAP for Me

I recommend the following:

  1. Check whether the required fields exist under Settings --> Columns in Analyse Stock in Date Range.                                                                           
  2. If they are not available, document the gap with screenshots.
  3. Use Custom CDS Views and Custom Analytical Queries to build a custom report for receipt value and issue value.                                                                                                 
  4. Classify the value logic by movement type, stock change direction, amount, currency, and reversal logic.
  5. Reconcile the custom report with Analyze Stock in Date Range.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Integration Suite sharing within company group - how to organize content
entry.content:
Hi all,

I'm working for a company which is part of a group. Until now, each company of the group uses several CPIs on NEO. We will need to migrate to Integration Suite now. For costing reasons, there is one global account owned by the group and shared with all companies. The Integration Suite landscape will consist of two subaccounts (test and prod). These subaccounts will be shared with all companies of the group due to costing reasons.

As same IS subaccounts will be used by various companies, administration and development as well as monitoring will be handled by different people at different locations and working for different companies. If we do this in an uncontrolled manner, it will become a mess. 

We need to find a way on how to organize all the content, artifacts, partner details etc. in a meaningful manner. For this, I want to define some governance of how to organize artifacts, naming conventions, authorization rules etc.

As I'm quite new to IS, I'm struggling with defining such a governance. I found lots of learning content but most of them are somewhat of fundamental. Create one project, create first flow and that's it.
What I've learned so far is that there is no hierarchy within integration projects, like we do have in ABAP packages. As projects are the most fundamental part of organizing content, I wonder if we should organize it by company, integration scenario (CPI for C4C, C4s, S/4, non-SAP), business area (Sales, Production) or in a different way?

I didn't found any best practices and for this, I want to ask you to point me into the right direction. How do you handle complex integration landscapes? Do you have some link to a good resource to best practices?

entry.title: Re: How to Restrict Users from Accessing Customer Invoice...
entry.content:
Hello,

It is currently possible to restrict user access to Customer Invoices and Supplier Invoices only at the Company level. At present, there is no option to restrict access based on Business Residence or Site level.

This is the expected and standard system behavior.
For more information, please refer to SAP Note KBA 3426144, "How To Set Access Restriction Rights for User on Org Unit Level?" This KBA also illustrates the current system behavior regarding access restrictions.

If you require this functionality, it would be considered a new enhancement request. Please submit your idea through the SAP Influence Portal:
# https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/886 


Kind regards,
Pavithra

entry.title: SAF‑T (PT) Payments/Clearings: Why scenarios with more than one Business Partner are not allowed
entry.content:
This blog explains why certain payment scenarios cannot be represented compliantly in SAF‑T (PT) 1.04_01, how this impacts SAP customers today, and what the SAF‑T XSD schema explicitly enforces when it comes to Payments, Customers, and Suppliers. 

Problem Scope 

The issue under discussion affects a very specific but impactful set of scenarios related with incoming manual payments and clearing transactions. 

Typical business cases:  

  • A single payment that settles open items for multiple customers 
  • Clearing transactions between customer and vendor accounts 

 

 

Technical Deep Dive: What the SAF‑T XSD Actually Allows 

To understand why this happens, we need to look directly at the SAF‑T (PT) 1.04_01 XSD schema, not at business expectations. 

 

 

Can a Payment Contain More Than One Customer?No - This is explicitly forbidden by the schema

The XSD defines a key reference constraint that binds each <Payment> to exactly one <CustomerID>: 

XML 

<xs:keyref name="PaymentPaymentRefNoCustomerIDConstraint" 

refer="CustomerIDConstraint"> 

<xs:selector xpath="ns:SourceDocuments/ns:Payments/ns:Payment" /> 

<xs:field xpath="ns:CustomerID" /> 

</xs:keyref> 

 A single SAF‑T Payment cannot represent a settlement covering multiple customers, even if the payment exists as one document in SAP. 

 

 

Can a Payment Reference a Supplier? No - Suppliers are not permitted in the Payment structure

Evidence from the Schema 

The Payment section includes the following constraints: 

  • PaymentPaymentRefNoConstraint 
  • PaymentPaymentRefNoCustomerIDConstraint 

There is no: 

  • SupplierID 
  • SupplierIDConstraint 
  • Alternative party element 

This design clearly indicates that: 

  • Payments are customer‑only constructs 
  • Supplier involvement is not supported, neither instead nor in addition to customers 

A SAF‑T Payment cannot reference suppliers, and any clearing between customer and vendor accounts inherently violates the schema. 

 

Closing Thoughts 

Understanding what the SAF‑T schema allows—not what we wish it allowed—is essential for designing compliant solutions. While SAP can improve validations and guidance, certain business scenarios will always require process alignment or redesign to remain SAF‑T compliant. 

Until preventive checks are introduced, customers should be actively discouraged from using multi‑party payment and clearing scenarios when SAF‑T reporting is required. 

 

Cheers, Elsa

entry.title: Project Budget Inquiry – Grouping by Project Type in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I have a question regarding Project Budget inquiries in Forge/ANA Public Cloud, and I haven’t been able to find a solution.

We use the Project Type field to categorize our projects. This field is configured as a selectable list, allowing users to assign a specific project type to each project.

The issue comes when analyzing project budgets. What I need is a report or inquiry that shows all projects with their Committed Costs and Actual Costs, grouped by Project Type.

In the budget inquiry application, I can use Project Type as a filter at the top of the screen, but I cannot group the results by this field in the output. As a result, if I have 10 different project types, I would need to run the same inquiry 10 times and manually consolidate the information.

Is there any way to:

Group budget inquiry results by Project Type?
Include Project Class as a grouping field in reports or inquiries?
Create a faster or more efficient solution to analyze committed and actual costs by project type?

Also, if the standard Project Class field cannot be used for grouping or reporting purposes, I would be open to creating a custom Z field and using that instead

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Portugal – Support for Original / Duplicate / Triplicate Identification in Billing Document Output
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

We would like to request clarification regarding the legal document output requirements in Portugal.

According to our customer's business and compliance requirements, billing documents (Invoices, Credit Notes and similar fiscal documents) must be printable in multiple copies identified as:

  • Original
  • Duplicate
  • Triplicate

Additionally, when a document is reprinted, it should be possible to identify the corresponding copy version accordingly.

At the moment, we have not identified a standard functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that allows the automatic identification of these document copies within the output form.

Could you please clarify:

  • Whether this functionality is currently supported in standard;
  • Whether there are any recommended approaches to achieve this requirement;
  • Whether this requirement is already known by the Localization or Output Management teams.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

entry.content:
Good morning experts,

After moving from gATP to aATp, we have been trying to implement a solution for Product Substitution 1:M AND which is a missing functionality provided by standard. This functionality is supported only by eSPP with the so-called "Sets" but not with aATP. For that reason, we are trying to find a solution by enhancing the existing aATP checks. We are thinking in adapting the table behind the master data - product substitutions (MDSUB) to also store 1:M AND substitution records and enhance the ATP checks to consider these during order entry. 

However, we are facing some issues trying to find the right spot where we should include our changes. According to SAP 3083222 - User exits ATP00001 in context of advanced ATP - this user exist should be avoided. Any idea where we could place our trigger point or has anyone faced a similar scenario with aATP in S4HANA?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Incorrect Planned Order Quantity Calculation in MD11...
entry.content:
Why this happens is because Scrap percentage (MRP1 view) is designed to ensure sufficient net requirement coverage. When you run MRP, the system calculates backward from the requirement, factoring in scrap, then applies lot-sizing rules. In MD11, however, you are entering the order quantity directly, and SAP adds scrap on top of it rather than recalculating against the fixed lot size.

If you want MD11 to behave like MRP, you need either enhancement / User Exit to adjust quantity calculation during planned order creation (e.g., MD_PLDORD_POST) or Process alignment and encourage users to create planned orders via MRP runs rather than manually, if consistency is critical.

entry.title: Re: Can AI Agent replace IT Architects (EA/SA)?
entry.content:
Actually, the irony is that while SAP keeps debating “whether AI agents can replace architects,” the top 5% of the industry has already moved on. It was the same back in the 90s, when we were debating if our cloud CRM iDECS would replace Excel.

Agentic AI with deep architecture skill‑packs — governance, modeling, integration, security, regression mapping — is already automating 92% of ERP customization work in firms like Accenture, PwC, NextERP, Odoo, and others who operate without the legacy bloat.

Claude Mythos is collapsing multi‑year SAP/ERP development cycles into hours, generating full blueprints, extensions, tests, and impact analyses before a human even touches the spec. Architects aren’t being replaced — the manual SAP-era sludge around them IS.

This isn’t hype. It’s just that the frontier is moving faster than the siloed, over‑engineered, data‑bloated ecosystems that still think “AI is coming someday.”

AI isn’t coming for architects. It’s coming for the fat bloat and unstructured siloed data, that made architecture slow.

entry.title: Source storage bin XX cannot be found in warehouse XX in Record defect app
entry.content:
Faced this issue in record defect app of S4 system. 

Here I was expecting the storage bin in Decentralized ewm will be dispalyed in F4. But its not the case. It can only be manually entered. Also was getting this error message, even the storage bin is available in decentralized EWM - In Warehouse Defect with Decentralized EWM | SAP Help Portal its mentioned that, if the user dont have the access then system will throw the error. But for my user id there is no access issue.
Later got to know that the S4 will use RFC user in decentralized EWM to validate the storage bin in EWM from S4. So the user maintained in SM59 for the EWM system to be tested for access issue SU53. 

 

RFC_TYPE FUGR RFC_NAME QFEWM
RFC_TYPE FUNC RFC_NAME QFEWM_GET_STORAGEBIN_INFO

Once maintained the access to that user in EWM the validation works fine in record defect app.

entry.title: SAP BTP vs. Power Platform
entry.content:
Two Languages, One Architecture — Pointing to Industry 6.0

Everyone frames them as competitors. They're not!
Microsoft Power Platform and SAP BTP are two dialects of the same architectural philosophy:
→ Push intelligence to the edge.
→ Dissolve data silos.
→ Turn operational noise into real-time decisions.
→ Let the people closest to the problem build the solution.
The mapping is almost symmetrical:
Power Apps ↔ SAP Build Apps
Power Automate ↔ SAP Build Process Automation
Dataverse ↔ HANA Cloud
Power BI ↔ SAP Analytics Cloud
Azure IoT ↔ SAP Digital Manufacturing + Edge Services
Different SDKs. Same conviction: The factory floor, the supply chain, and the design studio deserve the same composability that software engineering has had for 20 years.

Industry 5.0 — Skipped
Industry 4.0 gave us connectivity. Industry 5.0 demands intentionality:
- Human-in-the-loop creativity
- Regenerative supply chains
- Systems that adapt to people
- Technology that amplifies craft

Most enterprise platforms optimise processes. Very few optimise purpose.
They automate workflows — but not wisdom. They scale operations — but not human imagination. This is the GAP.

So We Built the Missing Layer.
After 20+ years of integrating cloud ecosystems, stitching platforms together, and watching the same fragmentation repeat itself, we arrived at a simple conclusion:
No existing system could unify creativity → supply chain → manufacturing → marketing → sales → support into one living, self-evolving operating layer.

So we built AuraOS, ready for Industry 6.0
Not a tool. Not an app. A semantic operating system — where data, agents, workflows, and human creativity run as one continuous organism.

AuraOS in Action: Sketch → Fabrication in One Agentic Flow
Today, AuraOS deploys the Sketch2Fabrication pipeline:
Signal → Idea → Concept → Material → Machine → Artifact → GTM
From a hand-drawn sketch to a market-listed product — orchestrated by:
- Semantic understanding across every stage
- Multi-agent collaboration with human-in-the-loop checkpoints
- Sustainable material logic is baked into the decision tree
- Closed-loop feedback from production to design
- A regenerative, circular manufacturing mindset
This is Agentic AI, not as a feature. It's an operating philosophy.

So What?
The organizations that will define Industry 6.0 won't be the ones choosing Power Platform or SAP BTP.
They'll be the ones building cohesive, edge-to-cloud ecosystems — where data, automation, creativity, and intelligence flow without friction.

That's the work I love: Designing architectures that unify operations, elevate teams, and turn complexity into clarity. Because in the end, platforms evolve. Architecture endures.

What's your experience bridging MPP/SAP in manufacturing?

##IndustryArchitecture #SAPBTP #AgenticAI
#EdgeToCloud #DigitalManufacturing #SmartFactory

entry.title: Clarification on Outbox Behavior in Multi-Instance Deployments
entry.content:
Questions:

  1. Worker thread distribution: Are worker threads created per application instance, or is there a global pool shared across all instances?

  2. Multi-instance coordination: How do workers across multiple application instances coordinate to avoid processing the same outbox message? 

  3. Ordered outbox guarantees: For ordered outboxes, how is sequential processing per tenant enforced when workers span multiple application instances?

  4. Resource planning: With thousands of tenants in a multi-instance deployment, what's the recommended approach to sizing worker pools to balance throughput and resource consumption?

  Context:

  We're running a multi-tenant application with horizontal scaling and need to understand the threading model and coordination mechanisms to properly plan capacity and ensure correct outbox processing behavior.

Thanks,

-Vinay.

entry.title: SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) | Customer Roundtable | June 2026
entry.content:
CAP Customer Roundtable

We’re excited to invite you to our second CAP Customer Roundtable of 2026 - your opportunity to engage directly with the CAP product team and fellow practitioners to discuss recent developments and future directions.

Join us this June to reconnect with the CAP community, exchange insights, and get ready for reCAP 2026.

 

 

What to expect

The session will include:

  • The latest CAP announcements and updates
  • Product demos and presentations
  • An open forum for your topics, questions, and discussions

 

 Agenda

  1. Introduction
  2. Updates from the CAP Team
  3. Elevate Your CAP Development: Seamless External Data Mashups for Enterprise (Rakesh Jain from CSC)
  4. Q&A / Topics by the Community

 

Event Details

  • Date: Wednesday, June 17, 2026, 10:00 AM CET
  • Location: Microsoft Teams
  • Meeting link: (will be shared soon)

 

Who should attend

  • All interested partners and stakeholders are invited to join: developers, enterprise architects, technical consultants…
  • The SAP Cloud Application Programming Model team
  • Anyone interested in learning about the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model and exchange your ideas and feedback with other users and the product team

 

Help Shape the Discussion

We want this roundtable to be as relevant as possible for you. Have topics, challenges, or questions you'd like us to cover? Let us know in advance - we’d love to include them!

 

Stay Connected

Also check out the events page in our documentation and our community page for updates on upcoming events.

 

We’re looking forward to seeing you there!

entry.title: Re: Custom field in IQ02/IQ3
entry.content:
Hi @mromik , you are not wrong, you are missing one of the step, don't know which one so I am writing all step so you can take care.

Step‑by‑Step Guide to Make CI_EQUI Fields Visible in IQ02/IQ03

  1. Activate CI_EQUI

Go to SE11 → CI_EQUI → Activate If it’s already active, move to the next step.

  1. Check the Screen Sequence

This is the part most people miss.

  1. Go to OIR4 – Define Screen Sequences
  2. Open the screen sequence used by your equipment category (usually 01)
  3. Look for the tab called Customer Fields
  4. Make sure the subscreen is set to: Program: SAPLXEQI Screen: 0200

If this subscreen is missing, add it.

  1. 3. Assign Screen Sequence to Equipment Category
  1. Go to OIRH
  2. Select your equipment category (like M, P, T, etc.)
  3. Make sure it is linked to the screen sequence that contains the Customer Fields tab.
  1. 4. Test in IQ02

Open IQ02 → Equipment → Extras → Technical Objects → Display Views You should now see Customer Fields in the list.

entry.title: Designing Controlled Case Routing in SAP ESM: Restricting Service Team Selection by Case Type
entry.content:
Use Case:

In Enterprise Service Management, organizations extend support across functions like Finance, Procurement, and Application teams

  • Requests are raised using predefined case types to streamline service handling
  • Each case type is intended to route requests automatically to the appropriate team
  • The objective is to ensure accurate, consistent, and efficient routing with minimal manual effort

Example Inputs

  • Invoice mismatch → Finance Case → Finance Team
  • Purchase order issue → Procurement Case → Procurement Team
  • System access issue → Application Case → App Support Team

The Challenge

  • Multiple service teams handle requests, but team selection is often manual
  • Users choose case types without understanding backend routing, ❌ leading to guesswork
  • This results in ❌ incorrect team assignments and ❌ frequent ticket re-routing
  • Ultimately, it causes ❌ SLA breaches and a ❌ poor user experience

 Solution Approach:

SAP ESM offers flexible configuration, but lacks a single standard mechanism to tightly control Case Type → Category → Service Team mapping

This solution introduces a scalable design pattern using:

  • Service Catalog
  • Determination Rules
  • Dynamic Properties
  • Validation Rules

Steps:

Design Flow: Case Type → Auto-Derived Category → Validation Rule → Allowed Service Team

  • Users choose Case Type and Service team
  • System controls everything else

Step 1: Design Service Catalog

Navigate to -->System Settings--> Service Catalog -->Select Entity -->Case -->Define functional Hierarchy--> Catalog Activate Category

Step 2: Map Service Catalog to Case Types: To ensure only relevant categories are used

  • Assign relevant catalog to Case Types
  • Activate configuration

Navigate to-->System Settings-->Case-->Case Type--> Assign Catalog-->Activate Case Type

Step 3: Automate Categorization (Determination Rules): To reduce dependency on user input

Navigate to--> System Settings-->Extensibility Administration-->Case Service-->Case-->Determination

Define logic: Example

  • IF Case Type = Finance Case → Category = OTC
  • IF Case Type = Procurement Case → Category = COUPA

Step 4: Lock Category (Dynamic Properties) as per case type: To make classification system-driven

Navigate to--> System Settings-->Extensibility Administration--> Case Service-->Case-->Dynamic Property

Set Category field: Hidden ✅, Read-only ✅, Required ✅

Step 5: Enforce Service Team Restriction (Validation Rules): To Ensures only valid combinations are allowed

Navigate to-->System Settings-->Extensibility Administration-->Case Service-->Case-->Validations

Define rules such as: Example

IF Category = OTC

AND Service Team ≠ Finance Team

Then Error

Step 6: Add User-Friendly Error Messaging: To Improves user behavior without training dependency

Example: “Case Type contains CA – please ensure an CA-relevant team is selected before saving.”

Outcome

  1. System blocks irrelevant team selection during case creation with clear validation messages
  2. Users cannot submit requests with incorrect team selection
  3. Ensures every case is consistently routed to the correct functional owner

Key Insights:

  •  Always derive classification systemically rather than manually
  • Use validation rules as governance checkpoints
  • Align catalog design with the business operating model
  • Avoid overloading users with decision-making
  • Keep logic scalable for future case types

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

Issue

Root Cause

Fix

Validation not working

Incorrect ID

Use UUID/Technical ID/Display ID

Wrong routing

Rule logic mismatch

Revalidate conditions

Manual override possible

Missing dynamic properties

Lock fields properly

Poor user adoption

Confusing error messages

Improve messaging

Conclusion

  1. Ensures accurate first-time routing and improves SLA adherence
  2. Reduces rework, reassignment, and operational inefficiencies
  3. Drives process standardization and improve service quality
  4. Enables a seamless and scalable user experience

entry.title: Release Assessment and Scope Dependency for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: What is new in 2026
entry.content:
Introduction

In 2026, we continue our commitment to delivering disruption-free upgrades and zero Day 1 impact for customers using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. With ongoing enhancements to the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency tool (RASD), we are further strengthening our ability to proactively identify and communicate all relevant Day 1 impacts based on customer usage.

Stay tuned as we bring you the latest updates and innovations in Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) throughout 2026.

Key Features and Enhancements in 2026

Change History

The Change History feature allows users to track updates made to the What’s New content for a specific release, from the Preliminary version through to the Release to Customer (RTC) version. Since preliminary content is typically published 3–5 weeks prior to RTC, this feature provides visibility into all updates made during that period.

It enables users to:

  • Stay informed about the latest changes
  • Minimize last-minute effort during RTC
  • Improve overall planning and readiness

How to Access and Use Change History            

  1. Navigate to Change History
    The Change History information published in the What’s New Viewer can be accessed directly via the link available at the top of the Complete What’s New Viewer Tab.


  2. Use the “Last Modified On” Filter
    Within each What’s New Viewer card, the “Last Modified On” filter enables users to:
    • View items updated after a selected date
    • Focus only on recent changes
    • Avoid rechecking previously reviewed entries

    Each card also includes direct navigation to the Change History content, allowing users to review updates at a more granular level.



    Rather than reviewing all entries, users can apply a date filter to focus on recent updates.

    This approach helps to avoid revisiting previously reviewed items and ensures efficient tracking of the latest changes.

entry.title: Unable to Update Sales Order Item Pricing Condition via API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am trying to update the pricing of an existing sales order item in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition via OData API.

Scenario:

  • Sales Order Type: Standard Order
  • The sales order has already been created successfully.
  • Afterwards, I would like to change the price condition of an item using the API.

I am using the following PATCH request against API_SALES_ORDER_SRV:

https://myXXX.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrderItemPrElement(SalesOrder='278',SalesOrderItem='10',PricingProcedureStep='20',PricingProcedureCounter='1')

Request Body:

{
  "d": {
    "ConditionType": "ZPR0",
    "ConditionRateValue": "0",
    "ConditionCurrency": "EUR"
  }
}

The PATCH request is executed successfully and no error is returned. However, the condition value in the sales order is not updated.

Is it possible to update pricing conditions of an existing sales order via API_SALES_ORDER_SRV in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

Thanks in advance for your help!

Katharina

entry.title: Re: Is LLM fine-tuning really dead?
entry.content:
That maps closely to how I think about it, with one addition @Marla-Ellinger.

Your triggers are mostly about reliability and cost: brittle prompts, expensive failures, and retry overhead. The factor that tips me most is slightly different. It's not the failure rate but rather where the failure lands.

A prompt that fails loudly and gets caught by schema validation is usually survivable. The cases that push me toward adapting the model are the silent failures: outputs that are perfectly schema-valid but quietly wrong. Validation can't catch those, so the error crosses the boundary into downstream systems.

When the cost of a wrong-but-well-formed answer is high, retries and guardrails stop being enough. That's usually my signal to adapt the model.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
entry.content:
“Most CPI developers configure adapters. Few understand what happens after deployment."

This independent research project exists to fill that gap: opening the adapter runtime box and helping the SAP Community understand what really happens inside SAP Cloud Integration after an iFlow is deployed. - Ricardo Viana, 03 June 2026

Part II — What Happens After iFlow Deployment: Consumer Runtime, Polling and Offset Commit

Most SAP Cloud Integration developers use adapters every day.

Very few understand what happens inside one after an iFlow is deployed.

This article opens that runtime box.

In Part I, I introduced the motivation and architecture behind EventSmartKafka, an open-source custom Kafka adapter for SAP Integration Suite / Cloud Integration.

Part II does not focus on Maven setup, project generation, OSGi packaging, or how to create the adapter project from zero. That belongs to Part III.

This article focuses on the runtime behavior behind the CPI channel:

  • how metadata.xml defines the adapter UI
  • how UI fields become Java Endpoint properties
  •  how the Consumer is created after iFlow deployment
  • how doStart() initializes the Kafka client
  •  how doPoll() reads Kafka records
  • how payload validation and Avro conversion happen before the CPI pipeline
  • how poison messages are handled and why offset commit must happen only after successful processing.

Kafka connectivity is only one part of the story.

The real adapter problem is controlling runtime behavior without silent payload loss, uncontrolled retry loops, hidden offset commits, or generic monitoring errors.

What to Expect: The 7-Part Deep-Dive Journey

To fully unpack the engineering behind an enterprise-grade SAP adapter, this initiative is structured into seven comprehensive technical chapters:

Part

Title

Focus

I 

The Journey & Architecture Overview

Project vision, metadata configuration, visual logging frameworks, and open-source delivery roadmap. 

II ★

Core Consumer Lifecycles & Polling Loops

Java runtime deep dive: how the Apache Camel polling consumer lifecycle maps to Kafka cluster subscription models. (This article)

III

The Multi-Profile Security Engine

Technical breakdown of SASL, mTLS, and OAuth 2.0 dynamic token integration using the SAP Secure Store.

IV

Multi-Threaded Parallel Consumers & Scale

Engineering for high-throughput. Parallel threading pools inside Cloud Integration worker nodes without JVM memory leaks.

V

Advanced Payload Conversion & Schema Registry Governance

Avro-to-JSON/XML serialization layer, caching strategies, and memory protection mechanisms.

VI

Designing the Producer (Receiver) Integration Role

Outbound flow: partition keys, acknowledgment levels (acks), and asynchronous message dispatching.

VII

Visual Operations, Error Interception & Production Sign-Off

Custom error UI rendering, transaction boundary management, and production go-live best practices.

Status: Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview

Before going deeper, I want to make the positioning explicit.

EventSmartKafka is being released as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

It is:

  • Not SAP-certified;
  • Not enterprise stress-tested;
  • Not SLA-backed;
  • Not a commercial product;
  • Not a replacement for vendor-supported Kafka adapters.

The objective is educational, maybe with future contribution can be something interessting for all.

The goal is to help the SAP Community understand what happens inside a custom adapter runtime after an iFlow is deployed.

The first public baseline focuses on the Consumer side — implemented as a CPI Sender Adapter:

  • Kafka event ingestion
  • raw passthrough
  • Avro to JSON conversion
  • Avro to XML conversion
  • Schema Registry integration
  • Confluent Magic Wire Format
  • Fixed Schema ID resolution
  • SASL / PLAIN
  • SCRAM-SHA-256
  • SCRAM-SHA-512
  • TLS connectivity
  • controlled runtime safety limits
  • compact CPI monitoring errors
  • poison message handling
  • offset commit control.

Producer-side validation, Protobuf, OAuth 2.0, and full mTLS validation remain roadmap items for later parts.

Production usage requires independent validation by the adopting organization.

Package Overview in SAP Integration Suite

Figure 1 — EventSmartKafka package inside SAP Integration Suite, positioned as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

SAP ADK - The open box for SAP SCN Community

SAP adapters are often treated as black boxes.

Developers configure them, deploy iFlows, and wait for messages to arrive — but very few see what happens inside the adapter runtime.

With the SAP Adapter Development Kit, that box can be opened.

A custom adapter exposes the real internal layers behind a CPI channel: metadata.xml, Component, Endpoint, Consumer lifecycle, polling logic, payload handling, error strategy, and offset control.

The objective of this article is not only to show EventSmartKafka as a Kafka adapter.

The objective is to open the box and make the adapter runtime understandable for the SAP Community.

Figure 2 — SAP ADK opens the adapter black box: from design-time metadata to runtime Consumer behavior.

SDIA and ODCP — The Invisible Domain Layer

EventSmartKafka is also an implementation example of SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture — and ODCP — Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern for SAP CPI.

SDIA does not replace tools, platforms, or existing governance models. It changes the operating model. The same artifacts remain: APIM, CPI, Kafka topics, metadata, security objects, iFlows, scripts, and backend services. What changes is the discipline used to organize and operate them.

When applied correctly, SDIA creates an invisible Domain Layer above the integration stack. This layer gives business meaning, ownership, boundaries, and operational control to technical artifacts across APIM, CPI, Kafka, and backend systems.

SDIA is a free and open architecture released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International — CC BY 4.0.

That is the principle behind SDIA: technology executes, but the domain governs.

Zenodo: Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA): A Vendor‑Agnostic Semantic Governance Model for Enterprise Integration - v3.0 - FINAL

Zenodo: Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern (ODCP): A Vendor-Agnostic Domain-Semantic Orchestration Topology for Enterprise Integration Platforms - v3.0 - FINAL

Figure 13 — SDIA defines the invisible governance layer above technical artifacts, keeping domain ownership consistent across adapters, iFlows, metadata, Kafka topics, security, and runtime behavior.

Why the Metadata Comes First

In SAP Cloud Integration custom adapter development, the UI is not a secondary detail.

The metadata.xml file is the contract between the design-time UI and the Java runtime.

It defines:

  • adapter variants
  • sender/receiver direction
  • tabs
  • attribute groups
  • field labels
  • dropdown values
  • default values
  • visibility conditions
  • runtime parameter names

If the metadata exposes a parameter that the Java endpoint does not understand, the adapter archive may still deploy, but the iFlow can fail when the endpoint is created.

Figure 3 — From the medata.xml to adapter UI visual

This is one of the most common custom adapter problems:

  • ESA deploys successfully
  • Channel appears in the iFlow
  • iFlow deployment fail
  • Endpoint cannot be created
  • Unknown parameter or missing JavaBean property

That is why the metadata and the endpoint class must evolve together.

If the UI exposes this:

<AttributeReference>
    <ReferenceName>schemaRegistryHostAddress</ReferenceName>
</AttributeReference>

Then the endpoint.class must expose this:

@UriParam
private String schemaRegistryHostAddress;

public String getSchemaRegistryHostAddress() {
    return schemaRegistryHostAddress;
}

public void setSchemaRegistryHostAddress(String schemaRegistryHostAddress) {
    this.schemaRegistryHostAddress = schemaRegistryHostAddress;
}

The name must match.The type must be compatible.The getter and setter must follow JavaBean conventions.

No mismatch.No guesswork.No hidden alias unless intentionally supported.

For EventSmartKafka, the current Consumer UI is organized into five major tabs:

  • Connection
  • Processing
  • Conversion
  • Conversion Advanced
  • Advanced

Each tab exists for a reason.

The objective is not to expose hundreds of parameters. The objective is to expose the right parameters at the right level, with clear separation between connection, polling, conversion, and runtime behavior.

The metadata.xml Opened in the IDE

Figure 4 — Adapter metadata defines the design-time UI exposed inside the SAP Cloud Integration channel.

Endpoint Java Properties

Figure 5 — Endpoint Java properties must match the runtime parameter names defined in metadata.xml.

Result:

Figure 6 — Adapter UI of Authentication Methods that comes from the metadata.xml, read by java SdiaKafkaEndpoint.class after the compilation into .esa ( adapter form) 

The Component Class

The Component class is the adapter entry point.

Its job is not to process Kafka records.

Its job is to connect the adapter scheme with the Camel runtime and create endpoint instances.

Conceptually, it looks like this:

public class SdiaKafkaComponent extends DefaultComponent {

    @Override
    protected Endpoint createEndpoint(
            String uri,
            String remaining,
            Map<String, Object> parameters) throws Exception {

        SdiaKafkaEndpoint endpoint = new SdiaKafkaEndpoint(uri, this);
        setProperties(endpoint, parameters);
        return endpoint;
    }
}

The important line is this:

setProperties(endpoint, parameters);

This is where Camel maps URI/metadata parameters into Java endpoint properties.

If the metadata sends a parameter that the endpoint does not expose, the endpoint creation may fail.

That is why the Component is simple, but critical.

It is the bridge between:

SAP Cloud Integration channel configuration
           ↓
Camel parameter map
           ↓
Endpoint JavaBean properties
           ↓
Consumer runtime behavior

A good Component class should stay boring.It should create the endpoint, apply parameters, and avoid business logic.

  • Kafka logic does not belong here.
  • Avro logic does not belong here.
  • Schema Registry logic does not belong here.

Component Class

Figure 7 — The Component class creates the Endpoint and connects the adapter scheme with Apache Camel.

The Endpoint Class

The Endpoint class is the runtime configuration contract.

It receives, stores, validates, normalizes, and exposes the adapter configuration that comes from the SAP Cloud Integration channel UI.

For EventSmartKafka, the endpoint is responsible for configuration areas such as:

  • Kafka bootstrap servers
  • topic pattern
  • partitions
  • consumer group ID
  • authentication mode
  • SASL mechanism
  • TLS flag
  • credential aliases
  • Cloud Connector Location ID
  • conversion format
  • Schema Registry host
  • Schema Registry credential
  • Schema ID resolution mode
  • payload safety limits
  • poll timeout
  • fetch settings
  • retry settings
  • runtime timing parameters

The Endpoint should not poll Kafka.

The Endpoint should not decode Avro.

The Endpoint should not commit offsets.

The Endpoint should provide the Consumer with a clean, validated configuration model.

A production-minded endpoint should follow three rules:

  • Fail fast on invalid configuration
  • Normalize values once
  • Expose explicit getters for runtime classes

For example, if the user selects PROTOBUF while Protobuf is not implemented yet, the adapter must not silently pass the record as raw bytes.

Avro Convertor possibilities:

It must fail clearly.

A silent fallback is worse than an exception.

This is especially important in event-driven architecture because silent failures can corrupt downstream business behavior while the monitor still looks green.

Endpoint Configuration Blocks

Figure 7 — SdkaKafkaEndpoint.class

Figure 8 — The Endpoint class receives and validates channel configuration before the Consumer runtime starts.

The Consumer Class: Where Kafka Meets CPI

The Consumer is the heart of the first public baseline.

In EventSmartKafka, the first release is Consumer-first.

This means the main validated flow is:

Kafka topic
     ↓
EventSmartKafka Consumer Adapter
     ↓
SAP Cloud Integration iFlow
     ↓
Downstream processing

The Consumer maps the Apache Camel ScheduledPollConsumer lifecycle to the Kafka KafkaConsumer polling model.

At runtime, the Consumer must:

  • initialize configuration
  • resolve credentials
  • connect to Kafka
  • subscribe to topics or assign partitions
  • poll records
  • optionally convert payloads
  • create a Camel Exchange
  • send the Exchange to the CPI pipeline
  • commit the Kafka offset only after successful processing
  • handle failures without silent data loss

The simplified runtime flow is:

KafkaConsumer.poll()
        ↓
ConsumerRecord<String, byte[]>
        ↓
raw passthrough or Avro conversion
        ↓
Camel Exchange
        ↓
Integration Flow pipeline
        ↓
successful processing
        ↓
commit offset

Reading from Kafka is not the hard part.

The hard part is committing the correct offset at the correct time.

 Consumer Class

Figure 9 — The Consumer maps the Camel polling lifecycle to Kafka record consumption.

Kafka to CPI Runtime Flow

Figure 10 — Consumer-first runtime flow from Kafka polling to SAP Cloud Integration message processing.

Why Offset Handling Is the Real Problem

A Kafka consumer can read a record in a few lines of code.

That is not adapter engineering.

Adapter engineering starts when something fails.

The key question is:

When is it safe to commit the offset?

For EventSmartKafka, the principle is simple:

Do not commit the offset until the message is successfully delivered to the CPI pipeline.

For raw passthrough, this means:

  • Read record
  • Create Exchange
  • Send payload to CPI pipeline
  • Pipeline succeeds
  • Commit offset

For Avro conversion, this means:

  1. Read record
  2. Validate payload
  3. Resolve Schema ID
  4. Fetch schema
  5. Decode Avro
  6. Create JSON or XML
  7. Send payload to CPI pipeline
  8. Pipeline succeeds
  9. Commit offset

If Avro conversion fails, the offset must not be committed.

Otherwise, the consumer group moves forward and the failed record is lost from the integration process.

That is exactly what a serious Kafka adapter must avoid.

The current behavior model is:

Scenario Offset Commit

Raw payload delivered successfullyYes
Avro converted and delivered successfullyYes
Missing Confluent Magic ByteNo
Invalid Schema IDNo
Schema Registry errorNo
Raw payload above hard limitNo
Conversion payload above hard limitNo
CPI pipeline failure with Stop on ErrorNo
CPI pipeline failure with Skip Failed MessageYes

This is why offset management belongs in the adapter layer.

If every iFlow must solve this independently, governance disappears.

Figure 11 — Offset commit must happen only after successful conversion and pipeline delivery.

Poison Pills and Silent Commits: The Two Failure Modes

A poison pill is a bad Kafka record that can be polled but cannot be processed. The consumer remains alive, but if the offset is not committed and no controlled strategy is applied, the same bad record is read again and again, blocking the next valid offsets.

A silent commit is the opposite problem. It happens when the adapter commits the offset even though the record failed. The consumer moves forward, the monitor may look green, but the failed message is lost.

EventSmartKafka is designed to make this behavior explicit: failed conversions are visible in CPI monitoring, poison messages are controlled by the selected error strategy, and offsets are committed only when processing is considered successful.

Figure 12 — Poison pills and silent commits are opposite failure modes: one blocks progress, the other hides data loss. A serious adapter must make both behaviors visible and controlled.

After iFlow Deployment: Runtime Lifecycle ASCII

[ SAP Integration Suite (CPI) ] Deploys iFlow
                             |
                             v
               Custom Adapter ESA is loaded
                             |
                             v
               [ Apache Camel ] Creates Component
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaComponent.createEndpoint(...)
                             |
                             v
                 metadata.xml parameters
                     are mapped into
                SdiaKafkaEndpoint setters
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaEndpoint.createConsumer(...)
                             |
                             v
               SdiaKafkaConsumer is created
                             |
                             v
                         doStart()
             ├── Validate Endpoint Configuration
             ├── Resolve CPI Secure Store Credentials
             ├── Build Native Kafka Client Properties
             ├── Instantiate KafkaConsumer Client
             └── Subscribe to Topic / Assign Partitions
                             |
                             v
             ScheduledPollConsumer Loop Starts
                             |
                             v
                         doPoll()
             ├── KafkaConsumer.poll() -> ConsumerRecord
             ├── Validate Payload Size Limits (Max 20MB)
             ├── Optional Conversion (AVRO/Protobuf -> JSON)
             ├── Create Camel Exchange & Inject Headers
             └── Dispatch Exchange to CPI Pipeline
                             |
                             v
                    CPI iFlow Processing
                             |
                +------------+------------+
                |                         |
         [ YES ]|                         |[ NO ]
                v                         v
          commitSync()             Evaluate Error Strategy
                |                   ├── 🔁 Retry Attempt
                v                   ├── ⏭️ Skip Message
         Next Poll Cycle            └── 🚨 Stop Pipeline

Runtime Class Responsibility

  • SdiaKafkaComponent: Creates the Endpoint and maps Camel parameters.
  • SdiaKafkaEndpoint: Stores and validates channel configuration.
  • SdiaKafkaConsumer: Starts Kafka connection, polls records, creates exchanges, controls offset commit.
  • SdiaKafkaAvroConverter: Converts Avro binary payloads into JSON/XML when conversion is enabled.
  • SdiaSchemaRegistryClient: Fetches and caches schemas by schema ID.

Compact Error Rendering

A custom adapter should not hide runtime failures behind generic Java stack traces.

For EventSmartKafka, conversion and payload validation errors are rendered in a compact CPI monitoring format.

The objective is to show the relevant runtime context directly in the monitor:

  • topic
  • partition
  • offset
  • bootstrap host
  •  schema mode
  • schema ID when available
  • error code
  • root cause

The current demo baseline includes visible error rendering for scenarios such as:

  • Avro payload without Confluent Magic Byte
  • invalid or unexpected Schema ID
  • Schema Registry lookup failure
  • payload above the configured conversion limit
  • payload above the raw passthrough limit

This matters because poison messages must be visible, traceable, and controlled.

A failed conversion should not silently commit the offset and disappear from the integration flow.

Figure 13 — Compact CPI error for Avro payload without valid Confluent Magic Byte.

Figure 14 — Compact CPI error for payload above the configured conversion limit.

What Has Been Tested So Far

The current Consumer demo baseline was validated in controlled non-production environments using trial, free-tier, or limited cloud resources.

Validated scope includes custom ESA deployment, iFlow channel creation, Kafka consumer startup, SASL/PLAIN over TLS, SCRAM-SHA-256/512, raw passthrough, Avro to JSON/XML, Schema Registry integration, Confluent Magic Wire, Fixed Schema ID, compact CPI monitor errors, conversion failure without offset commit, payload limits, and schema response limits.

This is not enterprise stress testing. It would be unrealistic to claim that an independent open-source project, developed and tested by one person, has validated every broker, security profile, network condition, payload size, partition strategy, retry behavior, and failure scenario.

All broker validations used trial, free-tier, or limited environments. These tests prove functional behavior under controlled conditions, not enterprise-scale certification.

Validated connectivity:
Confluent Cloud, Redpanda, Aiven.

Explored / partially tested:
IBM Event Streams.

What Is Explicitly Not in the First ESA

The first public ESA is Consumer-only.

It does not include validated Producer / Receiver role, Protobuf conversion, OAuth 2.0 production validation, full mTLS production validation, enterprise-scale performance certification, long-running endurance testing, vendor SLA, or SAP certification.

This is intentional. A community release should not mix validated functionality with roadmap items.

Figure 15 — EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka.esa

What Part III Will Cover

Part III will explain how to create the custom adapter project from zero using SAP ADK and will provide the first downloadable .esa package on GitHub as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

Users will be able to deploy the ESA in SAP Integration Suite, create a test iFlow, configure the EventSmartKafka Sender Adapter, and validate the Consumer runtime behavior.

The release will include basic documentation, configuration guidance, known limitations, and controlled test instructions.

The complete Java source code will be opened progressively in the next parts of the series.

Figure 16 — From blog to code to integration: Part III will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled deployment and testing in SAP Cloud Integration.

Conclusion

Part I explained why EventSmartKafka exists.

Part II opened the runtime box and showed what happens after an iFlow is deployed: metadata.xml becomes UI, UI parameters become Endpoint properties, the Consumer starts, doStart() prepares the Kafka client, doPoll() reads records, and offset commit is controlled after processing.

The main lesson is simple: reading from Kafka is easy. Safe runtime behavior is the real adapter engineering challenge.

That means controlling poison pills, avoiding silent commits, exposing compact CPI errors, and committing offsets only when processing is considered successful.

EventSmartKafka remains a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview: not SAP-certified, not SLA-backed, and not a replacement for vendor-supported adapters.

Part III will explain how to create the SAP ADK adapter project from zero and will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled testing.

The journey continues in Part III.

Part I - SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I

Ricardo Viana
Independent Solo Researcher

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

#SAPIntegrationSuite #SAPCloudIntegration #Kafka #SAPADK #OpenSource #EventDrivenArchitecture #ApacheKafka #Avro #SchemaRegistry #CloudIntegration #SAPCommunity

entry.title: SAP Enable Now - Translation function stopped working
entry.content:
Hi Team,

Where can I find any technical information regarding the Translation function? I'm using Producer, licenced with the Machine Translation capability. We have been developing and translating for 3 months, but today, it stopped. Once I select the Machine Translation option in Tools, the system generates and reads objects as if it is busy with translating the course. Then it finishes, with no obvious error, but there is no translated course to be found. We have translated for all courses and they are all set to the same standards.
- where can I see an error log and how would I read it?
- Could it be a space issue on the SEN server?

-What type of troubleshooting can I do or where can I find more information. This is quite urgent.  Any suggestions would help.
